ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for ngoh hiang liver roll. Mapped to the nearest parent class 'pork food product' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate anchor. A more specific term for deep-fried pork liver beancurd roll does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (processed pork meat products) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for ngoh hiang or liver roll in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for ngoh hiang liver roll or equivalent deep-fried pork liver beancurd roll as of 2024.

allergens: gluten (possible from five-spice seasoning or binders)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of liver roll ngoh hiang is one piece weighing approximately 60g, equivalent to one sausage-shaped roll as commonly served at hawker centres or as part of a mixed ngoh hiang platter.

nutrient_source.title: Liver roll ngoh hiang — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for 'Liver roll ngoh hiang' (cr_id F06100019). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical Singapore source at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 9.5g/100g based on the known fatty acid profile of deep-fried pork liver and fatty pork mince; this specific sub-fraction is not separately reported in the HPB record and is documented as an approximation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Pork and Beancurd Products — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database: Fried Beancurd / Tofu-based Dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for liver roll ngoh hiang in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 50 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney GI Database classification system) based on analogous deep-fried beancurd-wrapped pork products. The beancurd wrapper contributes a moderate-to-low GI (~50–55 for soy-based products), while the pork liver and fatty meat filling have negligible direct glycemic contribution. The carbohydrate content of 14.8g/100g is primarily from the beancurd sheet and seasoning binders. Glycemic load per 60g serving = (50 × 14.8 × 0.60) / 100 = 4.4g. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 660mg/100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold; high saturated fat (~9.5g/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit liver roll ngoh hiang due to its sodium content of 660mg/100g, which exceeds safe thresholds; if consumed, restrict to one small piece and avoid sodium-containing dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total fat (28.9g/100g) and moderate carbohydrate (14.8g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.6g/100g) elevate postprandial glucose and worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption of liver roll ngoh hiang as its high fat content (28.9g/100g) slows but does not prevent postprandial glucose excursion, and its low fibre content (1.6g/100g) provides insufficient glycemic buffering; portion should be restricted to one piece and sweet dipping sauces avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5g/100g), deep-fried preparation generating dietary AGEs, estimated trans fat (0.1g/100g), and high sodium (660mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid liver roll ngoh hiang due to its combination of high saturated fat (~9.5g/100g), elevated sodium (660mg/100g), and deep-fried preparation which generates advanced glycation end-products and trace trans fats that are atherogenic and pro-inflammatory.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs; pork liver is high in arachidonic acid; estimated trans fat (0.1g/100g) amplifies inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid liver roll ngoh hiang, particularly during flares, as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, pork liver contributes arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways, and trace trans fats further worsen systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Liver roll ngoh hiang (猪肝五香卷, also known as Wu Xiang Juan) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese deep-fried snack of Hokkien and Teochew origin. It is made by combining minced pork liver with fatty pork mince, seasoned with five-spice powder, garlic, egg, and cornstarch, then tightly wrapped in dried beancurd sheet (tofu skin) to form a sausage-shaped roll before being deep-fried until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy from the fried beancurd wrapper, while the interior is dense, savoury, and rich with the characteristic iron-forward flavour of pork liver balanced by the aromatic warmth of five-spice. Ngoh hiang is commonly served at hawker centres, zi char stalls, and festive occasions as part of a mixed platter alongside other fried items such as prawn rolls and fish cakes, typically accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or a fermented prawn paste dip. Nutritionally, liver roll ngoh hiang is energy-dense at 373 kcal/100g, with high total fat (28.9g), moderate protein (13.4g), and moderate carbohydrate (14.8g). Sodium is elevated at 660mg/100g, exceeding recommended thresholds for hypertension management. Iron content is notably high at 9.0mg/100g, reflecting the pork liver component. The deep-fried preparation contributes saturated fat (~9.5g/100g) and trace trans fats (~0.1g/100g). This food is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and caloric density. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the original record is erroneous and has been removed, as this product contains pork liver and fatty pork mince.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the exact food item 'Liver roll ngoh hiang' (cr_id F06100019) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is approximated from known fatty acid profiles of deep-fried pork liver and fatty pork mince as the HPB record does not separately report this sub-fraction. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists. All other values are exact from the approved primary source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5g/100g) is an approximation derived from the known fatty acid composition of deep-fried pork liver and fatty pork mince; the HPB Foodo record does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This value should be replaced with a directly measured figure when available from an approved source. 2. Trans fat (0.1g/100g) is an estimate based on deep-frying in vegetable oil; the HPB record does not report trans fat separately. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this specific food item, and no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact match for liver roll ngoh hiang with these micronutrient sub-fractions. 4. The dietary tag 'plant_based' present in the original input record is erroneous and has been removed; this food contains pork liver and fatty pork mince and is not plant-based. 5. GI of 50 is classified as LOW per the University of Sydney GI Database classification system (GI ≤55 = low; GI 56–69 = medium; GI ≥70 = high). This is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried beancurd-wrapped pork products; no direct GI measurement for liver roll ngoh hiang exists in the published literature as of 2026. gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match GI 50 under the University of Sydney classification. 6. Halal status is marked false as pork liver is a primary ingredient. 7. Calcium (20.0mg/100g) and iron (9.0mg/100g) are reported directly from the HPB Foodo source.
